text: The surgeon inserts a 10mm umbilical port and a 10mm port in the left iliac fossa for laparoscopy. The laparoscopic view reveals the right colon and transverse colon displaced to the left side due to the sickle volvulus, while the small bowel is positioned on the right side. Two additional ports, a 5mm port at the right iliac fossa and another at the epigastrium, are inserted.

The surgeon uses automatic graspers to gently de-twist the colon, which is initially twisted clockwise, by rotating it in an anti-clockwise direction. The cecum is repositioned to lie on the right side of the falciform ligament, and the left side of the transverse colon is elevated to create space for the small bowel.

The surgeon uses automatic graspers to carefully reposition the small bowel from the right side to the left side, allowing the cecum and right colon to lie in their anatomical positions without causing injury.
The surgeon performs a right hemicolectomy by making the ileocolic pedicle taut, followed by parmedial dissection and dissection in front of the duodenum.
The surgeon clips the ileocolic artery with a hammer lock and divides it using an end seal device. Medial dissection is then performed using blunt dissection techniques to mobilize the colon.

A tonsil swab is placed in front of the duodenum to serve as a marker for later retrieval. The surgeon then takes down the transverse colon and hepatic flexure, using the tonsil swab as a visual aid for orientation.

The surgeon mobilizes the appendix, cecum, ascending colon, and hepatic flexure from the lateral side, achieving full mobilization of the right colon. The bowel appears redundant but does not impede the dissection process.
The surgeon extracts the completely mobilized colon through the umbilical incision. The anastomosis is performed extracorporeally, outside the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs a relaparoscopy to visually inspect and clear any fluid accumulation within the abdominal cavity.
